comments: Special guest Rick Wakeman pops in for a chat and to play tracks from his new album.
No details are available for the second Bowie session. TV introduces it as being recorded at Maida Vale in Mono.
TV announces Tom Robinson Band as part of the line up at the start of the show but no track by them is in fact played in this edition .

sessions.details: recorded 1972-01-11 for John Peel (Session #1), first TX 1972-01-28. No known commercial release.

track_listing.track: ‘Sex Pistols Medley’ (LP-Great Rock And Roll Swindle) (Virgin)
A short chat with Rick Wakeman
‘Life Is Just A Passing Parade’ (LP-Oneness, Silver Dreams-Golden Reality) (CBS)
A short chat with Rick Wakeman
A short chat with Rick Wakeman
A short chat with Rick Wakeman

transcript.text: On 275 and 285, also on Stereo VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Hi there, this is Tommy Vance. Welcome to the Friday Rock Show.
Tonight, two classic sessions by David Bowie, recorded back in 1972.
Both sessions to be played uninterrupted, and both sessions not heard since 1972.
Also this evening, we feature mainly new cuts by The Skids, Bad Company, Manfred Mann's Earth Band, Frank Zappa, Black Arab, Supertramp and the Tom Robinson Band, among others.
And later, live in the studio, will be Britain's blonde bombshell of the keyboard, Rick Wakeman,
to play cuts from his new double album coming out in April.
Tonight, we start with The Rods.
And that was a track by The Skids from their new album, which is called Scared to Dance.
It's the title track.
And incidentally, The Skids and Magnum are going to be in concert tomorrow on Radio 1 at 6.30pm.
And before that, a track from the new album by Eddie and the Hot Rods,
as they now have started to recall themselves.
It's a track from their new album, which is called Thriller.
and it was called Strangers on the Payphone.
We were having a look at the sleeve
for the new album by Manfred Mann's Earth Band
which incidentally is called Angel Station
and on the sleeve notes it said
Hollywood Town and You Are I Am.
Any similarity between these two is purely intentional.
So we thought we'd do a montage of both of them
and maybe you can figure out
why they consider it so intentional.
Walking outside, feelings they hide
Putting their pride through well-known fates
And shining on cards
Their heads are high, but their hearts are low
Down, dragging as they go
Reaching out to those on the face
Tonight and tomorrow night, they're at Wembley
That's Bad Company
And Oh, Atlanta
For their new album, which is called Desolation Angels
And before that, a montage of two cuts
Which are on Angel Station
The new album by Manfred Mann's Earth Band
The two cuts were Hollywood Town
and you are I Am, and I bet you couldn't hear the join.
I certainly couldn't.
The similarity? Both use exactly the same backing tracks.
Shades of dub.
Not bad for a band, really, that started life as the Manhug Blues Brothers
in the early 60s, playing at Butlins.
Now let's get on to the first session by David Bowie.
This was recorded on the 11th of the 1st, 1972,
and it was used by John Peel way back in those days.
It has not been heard on Radio 1 since those particular days.
And it also, may I add, includes a track that he's never released ever on wax.
Have a listen.
And that's the track that, to the best of our knowledge,
has never been issued on record by David Bowie,
Waiting for the Man, the Lou Reed composition done by the Velvet Underground.
And before that, you heard from the rise and fall of Ziggy Stardust
and the Spiders from Mars, Hang On to Yourself, Five Years, and Ziggy Stardust,
recorded 11-1-72.
That was the year when Bowie became a superstar.
The band line-up at the time of recording was Mick Ronson on lead guitar,
Trevor Boulder on bass guitar, and Mick Woody Woodmansey on the drums.
And no matter what John Peel has to say on the air in terms of disparaging remarks
with regard to the people who listen to the Friday Rock Show,
as far as he thinks you're all balding and you've got false teeth,
I think it's safe to remember that Peely really was at the forefront of a lot of great music,
and probably still is.
Probably.
Here's Frank Zappa.
...motion picture and television engineer.
I first heard that cut, I honestly thought I was listening to a soundtrack of Seaside Special.
It's a medley of various songs by that amazing group that nearly were the Sex Pistols,
sung there by the Black Arabs, and it's included on the soundtrack double album of the great rock and roll Swindle,
which is just out on Virgin, which is more than they can say for the film.
I was reading in, I think, Melody Maker or Sounds about the film.
they need another 150,000 pounds to finish it,
but I'm sure we'll eventually see it.
And before that, you heard Frank Zappa,
two cuts from his new double album,
which is called Shake Your Booty,
Baby Snakes, and Trying to Grow a Chin.
Very rarely can I use the word masterpiece,
but I think I would apply it to the new album by Supertramp.
I was up before the dawn.
Norman Brothers Band there,
and a track from their new album,
which has just come out on Capricorn,
it's called Enlightened Rogues,
and the cut was called Try It One More Time,
a miracle that they've ever been revived.
They've had their share of motorcycle disasters,
show business marriages, and narcotics trials.
Forget over.
And before that, Supertramp on a track from their marvellous new album,
just out on A&M.
It's called Breakfast in America.
Four minutes to eleven on the Friday Rock Show,
and now comes the time for the Friday Night Connection.
But first, the answer to last week's Friday Night Connection.
Three tracks we played, Trapped by Todd Rundgren's Utopia,
Shining On by Grand Funk,
and Paradise by the Dashboard Light Part 1 by Meatloaf?
And the answer was Todd Rundgren produced The Lot.
And the first letter out of the bag, which was quite sizable,
was from Mr. C.N. McLean of No. 45 Dura Street, Dundee, DD465W.
C.N. McLean, you get yourself a £10 record token.
And for us, you put together your own Friday Night Connection.
And that's tonight's Friday Night Connection,
which was put together by Mr. C.N. McLean
at No. 45 Dura Street in Dundee.
Three pieces of music that have a very definite connection.
Now, if you think you know what they are,
well, jot the answer down on a postcard,
but you must put together your own Friday Night Connection as well
and send it along to me.
You might win yourself a £10 record token.
It goes like this.
Tommy Vance, Friday Night Connection,
BBC Radio 1, London, W1A 4WW.
And Mr. McLean, just as soon as we can get the money out of Tony Wilson.
you know how hard it is, will be sending you a £10 record token.
Just to answer quite a number of inquiries
about the piece of music that's going on in the background at the moment,
particularly the inquiry of Lucy Hines of Cedar Lodge,
Middleton Park, Middleton, Stoney and Oxen,
it's actually the Radio 1 theme by the Vandegraaff Generator.
Ten minutes after 11 o'clock, a pleasure to welcome into the studio
a man who's obviously had a haircut by the name of Rick Wakeman.
Where have the locks gone, Rick?
They went down a dustbin, I'm afraid.
It's called old age, you know.
So when you start approaching that magic age of thronatory,
you know, the locks have to go.
Yeah, down the dustbin in Switzerland.
I'm afraid so, yeah.
You're just over here to promote the new double album,
but also, I think, to set up a tour
that's going to happen in America soon, is that right?
Well, we're setting up a tour.
Funny enough, I come over here not to set up the...
really to do promotion for the album,
but mostly to help set up the tour for England,
because the American tour's already set up,
which starts beginning of April, April 1st,
goes through the end of June the 30th.
And we're doing some English dates in August
and some European dates
and a few provincial dates around England,
which is basically why I'm here.
That's with Yes, not as a solo thing?
That's with Yes.
And I'm going to do a few things on my own,
but basically I'm over here to do the Yes thing
and also to see Brentford play Chester tomorrow.
Yeah? You're still a football freak?
Oh, yeah.
I mean, I've come over here
because it's very nice to see Brentford's stuff
in six goals against Chester tomorrow.
Sorry for all those people living in Chester,
but there you go.
There you go.
OK, more with Rick Wakeman after The Tubes.
Whom do you like?
Very much so, yeah.
Well, here's a track from their new album.
We saw posters on the wall
Got myself a steady job
I keep seeing people happy everywhere
All I get to do is play my guitar
Carla Santana from the album that's just come out on CBS
called Deverdip Oneness.
And the track was called Life is a Laugh
and A Passing Parade.
And before that, something by The Tubes from their new album, Remote Control.
The track was TV is King.
And in our studio at the moment is Rick Wakeman.
Rick, I read in a paper the other day that one of the guys out of The Tubes
is being wheeled on in a wheelchair.
Do you know anything about that?
I don't know about the latest thing, he's being wheeled on in a wheelchair.
I think it's most probably Fee, who's the lead singer.
It doesn't surprise me, because he's been doing that for years.
He does this thing with a chainsaw where he jumps in the audience,
and every time he jumps in the audience, he normally breaks a leg or breaks an arm.
and they've got the great old principle, the show must go on.
And I've seen them go on with a broken arm and a broken leg,
so it doesn't surprise me.
I've seen them on stage about five times now,
and I have to say that I think they're one of the most exciting things
I've ever seen on stage in my life.
They never stop working, do they?
No, they graft really hard.
They're a credit to the industry, actually.
I think the last time I actually saw you was in a pool of frozen water.
Yes, I've done a bit of that.
Yeah, we all make mistakes, don't we?
That was a good gig, but I...
Enjoyed it. You know, I enjoyed it.
My bank manager didn't enjoy it.
You lost a lot of money on that, didn't you?
National Westminster Bank lost a lot of money on it.
That's true, yeah.
I'm with that bank.
Hang about, you probably lost all my money.
Yeah, well, there you go. That's why you're here.
That's right.
Going back to the early part of your career,
you worked on so many different sessions
after you were thrown out of college
or after you were dismissed from the Royal College of Music.
I've got here Cat Stevens, T-Rex and David Bowie.
I mean, who else did you work with?
Oh, I did everything, believe it or not, from Des O'Connor...
I can't say downwards, it has to be Des O'Connor upwards, really.
I mean, you name it, I did...
In the space of three years, I did about 2,500 sessions.
That's a lot. It's an awful lot, yeah.
I can't even remember half of the people that I worked on.
All I know is that I'm third in the record
behind a guy called Terry Cox, who's a drummer,
and behind Barry Morgan, who was also the drummer,
at one stage in the dear old Top 50, as it used to be then.
I was playing on 31 of the Top 50.
Barry was on about 33.
And Terry Cox, I think, still holds the record on 38.
Good heavens.
How many things did you do with Bowie?
You did Space Oddity.
Yeah, I did Space Oddity.
I did the whole of two other albums with him.
I did Hunky Dory and, well, there's one album he did,
and you may be able to help me out here, actually,
it was sold under so many different titles,
and it was Man Who Sold The World or whatever,
and it was released originally on Philips over here,
just, I think, called The Space Oddity Album,
which had a really freaky cover, a really great cover,
and I did around another dozen sort of sidetracks with him as well,
worked a lot with him.
He's now my next neighbour in Switzerland, which is quite interesting.
Well, you can settle back along with everybody else, if you wish,
and have a listen to some sessions that were done at the BBC Studios
on the 21st of the 9th, 1972, which, were you on or not?
No, before my time.
Got to say that, haven't I?
These are very interesting.
I was talking earlier with our Mr Wilson
as he was handing out the £10 for you to give away.
These are really quite collector's items, some of these.
They are.
I've never heard any of them.
Well, the studio versions I've not heard at all.
Well, settle back. Here they are.
David Bowie from 1972.
Three numbers there by David Bowie,
recorded the 21st 9th 1972 in the Maida Vale studios,
good old mono days they were,
couple that were on the rise and fall of Ziggy Stardust
and the Spiders from Mars, Lady Stardust and then Star,
and before that something that was issued as a single
called John, I'm Only Dancing.
Rick, what do you think of Bowie these days and what he's doing?
Well, I think in recent sort of months rather than say years,
I think he's started to get back to his old style of writing,
which is more of a poetic style,
which I think suits him an awful lot better.
I mean, I've heard some of the tracks that he's done on the new album,
which is not released yet, which Tony Visconti did,
because I'm now working with Tony Visconti,
so Tony played me some of the stuff that he's done.
And I was quite pleased, in fact,
that he's going back to the Ziggy Stardust, Hunky Dory days.
I think he's going into a bit of a rut,
same as maybe everybody does to a certain extent.
I was going to ask you, how difficult is it for a major established artist
to continue to at least appear to be creative?
It's very difficult because you get into a rut
where it's almost like a car manufacturer that produces a successful car
and then produces the same sort of model year after year after year.
Volkswagen Beetle is a classic example,
and then suddenly they have to do a new model
that's going to appeal to the people that are coming up now,
and often or not you have to go back to the old syndrome
that you were into, which is what you're best at
and there's no doubt about it.
Tony Visconti and David Bowie working together
on the poetic side of music is perfect
so I'm pleased to see him coming back to that now.
That's nice to hear.
Let's play a track from your new double album
which is coming out in April.
This is also going to be a single, I think, too.
Yeah, if you're playing the whole thing
then if you can imagine stopping halfway through
then you have to turn over the record
if you buy the single.
It's Rhapsody in Blue.
I think you might be laying yourself open
to a lot of criticism taking something on like that
because that is a classic bit of music.
Yeah, I know.
But I've always been open to criticism
in everything I've done.
That's true.
Yeah.
The idea actually came originally,
believe it or not, from my manager
who said that Rhapsody in Blue
is the classic piece of music
but when you actually sit down and listen to it,
it was written for the film
and it was taken from the film
and it has lots of up and downs and slowing ups
and it's not actually a complete piece of music
and the idea was Brian, my manager, said
why don't you try and put it together as one piece of music
that keeps a steady rhythm throughout from start to beginning
and see what you can do.
So we put down a back track
which was just basically bass, drums and guitar
and then we took the orchestral score
which we got kindly from Chappels
and using synthesizers
just put down every line, one after the other.
It took an awful long time to do, and I know it's open to criticism,
but I happen to enjoy making it, and I think it's a fine piece of music.
I'm not saying my version is a fine piece of music,
but it's a fine piece of music as Rhapsody in Blue.
So I enjoy doing it, and I'll stand up for it.
May I say that I think it's a first-class treatment?
That's very kind of you. What can I say?
Do you want cash or check?
I don't want anything. Keep making music.
Here's something that you wrote called March of the Gladiators,
which is also on the double album Rhapsodies.
Rick, do you use dubbing on your fingers?
No, I don't want to be football boots.
It doesn't help me football playing.
I think if I used it on my fingers,
it would not be playing and playing either, actually.
But you're so supple, I mean, you're so mobile.
Well, you're only saying that because you're sitting on my lap, Tom.
Don't give the secrets, aren't you?
Sorry.
You're an amazing technician.
Do you sometimes find that the technique of playing the instrument
cuts across the creativity?
It did for one period of time, I must own up, yeah.
I had about two years where I thought technique was more important than anything else.
And then I, through a lot of people, including my father,
who taught me a lot because he was a good piano player,
taught me to realise that technique is something that you can learn,
but then you use it to the best of your ability.
But you don't use it to show off or be flash.
But you can use it according to the music you make.
So in the last couple of years, music has taken over,
and I use the technique as an...
I can't really think of the right word,
but you use the technique you've got to employ within the music.
Sure, to give you expression.
Yeah, rather than the other way round,
which I did get into for about a couple of years, I must say now.
I was going to ask you the time-honoured hackneyed disc jockey question,
what sort of music do you like?
But then everybody's going to know
if they listen to Star Special on Sunday anyway.
Yeah, well, I did the Star Special thing, which comes out on Sunday,
and for the regular listeners of Star Special,
they realise that it has in fact reached an all-time low on Sunday.
There was a few tears, it was great fun.
I thoroughly enjoyed doing the programme actually
and I think it might come to quite a shock to some people
if they hear the sort of music that I like.
A couple of clues as to what you will be playing.
It's a mixture, it goes from everywhere from the Bonzos
through to Rick Derringer.
It goes through everything. It's a real mixture.
Radio 1, Sunday, 7 o'clock, Star Special, Rick Wakeman.
What a beautiful promo.
Do they still call you Uncle Rick?
Yeah.
It's not my fault.
Honest?
No, not really.
Something else you've chosen to put on Rhapsody
is this amazing Gershwin song, Tune Melody.
Summertime by Rick Wakeman, which is really beautiful.
I mean, that's a great thing to choose.
That's the type of melody that transcends any blooming era,
punk era, new wave, rock and roll, call it what you like.
Yeah, gentlemen, I'm a scholar for saying that.
That's true, though, isn't it?
Yeah, it used to be my party piece.
Every time I used to go to pubs or clubs
and somebody used to get up and play,
that's what I always used to play.
And I wasn't ever going to record it,
and Tony Visconti said, you've got to record it.
That's the only reason I did it, and I'm really glad I did, actually.
Rhapsodies is your eighth release, solo, right?
It doesn't have a theme, unlike most of the others.
Yeah, that's basically, again, a lot down to Tony Visconti
because he thought, and I think he was quite right,
that I was getting into a rut.
I was getting used to writing thematic things.
I knew how to do the various formats and put things together.
And he said, I ought to try and do something different,
make the track shorter, more accessible,
not to the public, but more accessible to me.
In other words, do all the things that I always wanted to do.
So this album basically is all the things
that I always really wanted to do.
I mean, there's lots of other tracks that are a mixture of...
There's reggae tracks, there's almost out-and-out disco tracks on it.
There's also very hard rock tracks.
There's a mixture of everything, all the things I really always wanted to do,
but I never really had the confidence on my own to do.
I needed somebody else to sort of give me a bit of a kick
and say, you know, do what you like.
I've really enjoyed doing it.
Excluding what you're doing now, what was the most...
the most sort of, if you like to use a posh word, fecund period of your life?
Which period of your life, excluding now, do you think was the greatest period of your life?
Most probably the Six Wives period, Six Wives and Journey period,
because there was absolutely no pressures, because I hadn't made records before.
On my own, I made records with other people and with Yes and with Straws,
but the Six Wives, Journey era, I could do, again, exactly what I liked,
because nobody had heard anything that I'd done,
and there were no rules or regulations I had to abide by.
You were setting a trend.
In many ways, you were.
Maybe to a certain extent, but it was not deliberate.
There was nothing deliberate about anything I did.
I think after those two albums, things went a bit astray,
to be honest, to a certain extent,
and now I've come back to not having any pressures
and doing what I'd like to do,
which I think is the fairest way about doing music.
One thing that you've always been known for is your great sense of humour,
and I would suggest that it's evident in the title of this.
This is called The Flasher.
Yeah, well, that's very much down to the BBC jocks
because I could never understand why you all had Rolls-Royces
but only at 30 quid a week until I went over Hyde Park
and you remember that Sunday morning that I saw you.
And so I wrote a track for you all, which is called The Flasher.
I just put on the plastic Mac and play it.
It's The Flasher from the album that's going to be coming out in April by Rick Wakeman.
Rick, thank you very much for coming into the studio.
It's very kind of you to invite me. I thank you very much.
It's nice to see your smiley face in dear old London town again.
What can I say?
Really.
The album's on A&M.
Yeah.
They think it is.
I think it is.
And it will be in April.
Thanks a lot, Rick.
Thanks very much.
Look forward to seeing you in Hyde Park.
Nice.
With the Mac.
Yeah.
And that's the Friday Rock Show for this week.
Many thanks for your company.
I hope you enjoyed the David Bowie sessions from 1972.
Our engineer tonight was John Morrison.
Thank you, John.
Our producer, as always, was the lovable Tony Wilson.
He did the lot and kept us all in gear.
Peebles and Peele, weeknights on Radio 1.
and Radio 1 is joining up with Radio 2 now
to take you through the night
or if you'd like something a little louder,
Rock Goes to College.
We'll be starting in five minutes' time on BBC 2.
A repeat from last Saturday,
Herbie Hancock from Chelsea College.
Wherever you are in our country,
take care of yourselves.
I'll see you next week.
Till then, God bless.
